text: fails to place the book within van Gennep’s academic and intellectual trajectory. After his death, Arnold van Gennep was ‘rediscovered’ in several stages. One of the most prominent thinkers to recognize his importance was Claude Lévi-Strauss. As noted above, van Gennep paid great attention to systems of exchange, and may have inspired both Mauss and Lévi-Strauss more than has so far been recognized, and more than both Mauss and Lévi-Strauss themselves ever conceded. In this vein, Senn has argued that van Gennep be recognized as a precursor to structuralism (Senn 1974: 242). However, Lévi-Strauss’ ‘rediscovery’ of van Gennep remained constrained by his structuralist approach and search for laws of logic. This was certainly miles away from van Gennep’s own attempt to establish a ‘biological sociology’, a science studying faits naissants, i.e. cultural phenomena at their moment of occurrence. Lévi-Straussian structuralism used finished texts (myths, kinship terminologies, cooking recipes) as its data. Liminality makes sense only within social dramas as they unfold. Despite their recognition of van Gennep, it is therefore not surprising that the concept of liminality was not at all taken up by Lévi-Strauss or any other member of the French or British structuralist schools, even as they slowly started to recognize van Gennep’s brilliance. It was Victor Turner who re-discovered the importance of liminality. It was one of Turner’s many merits to ‘liberate’ van Gennep’s framework from both the functionalist and structuralist straight-jackets, inserting van Gennep’s book on ritual passages where it truly belongs: in a processual approach. During his fieldwork, Turner had heard about van Gennep via the work of Henri Junod (Turner 1985: 159). However, his real encounter with van Gennep happened in 1963, and it conducted him toward his lifelong exploration into the liminal. We need now to engage with those explorations, in order to understand how, via the work of Turner, liminality made its way back into anthropology and the social sciences. Liminality and the Modern 76 Turner’s Encounter with Arnold van Gennep: From Social Dramas to Liminality Victor Turner (1920–1984) is without doubt one of the most fascinating and important anthropologists of the twentieth century. He always had an acute perception of the importance of threshold experiences. It is difficult not to think of a certain parallel with Arnold van Gennep in terms of formative life experiences. Van Gennep, we remember, lost his father after a sudden divorce and was then taken by his mother to live in another country at the age of six. More or less the same happened to Turner. Turner was born in Scotland, but at the age of eleven he was suddenly forced to leave as he went with his divorced mother to live with his maternal grandparents in Bournemouth, England. Turner later studied English language and literature at University College London (1938–1941). During the war he spent some time in a public library, and had a formative reading experience when he encountered Margaret Mead’s Coming of Age in Samoa. That famous book, as is well-known, describes the culture-specific ways in which children of either sex turn into adults. It was one of many attempts of the period to combine psychological perspectives concerning human personality with social analysis. Turner decided to study anthropology at University College of London. Here he attended lectures by some of Britain’s best anthropologists, including Daryll Forde, Meyer Fortes and Edmund Leach. He received his B.A. with honours in 1949. Max Gluckman, the leading member of the Manchester School, then offered Turner a grant from the Rhodes-Livingstone Institute to carry out fieldwork in an African tribe. Turner accepted and was assigned to the Mambwe tribe. In the event, he never reached the Mambwe homeland; during his stay at the Institute in Lusaka he received a telegram from Gluckman: ‘Suggest you change to Ndembu tribe Northwestern Province much malaria yellow fever plenty of ritual’ (E. Turner 1985: 2). In 1950, together with his wife Edith, Turner moved to the Mukanza village in the Mwinilunga district of Northern Rhodesia (now Zambia). Here Victor Turner started his fieldwork among the Ndembu, the ethnographic ground of his life’s work. Plenty of ritual indeed! Edith Turner was always with him, and it is well-known that she helped him greatly with data-gathering and its analysis. Turner had been trained in Marxist conflict theory and Durkheimian-inspired functionalist anthropology, and his early work largely stayed within this tradition, analysing schism and conflict as part of the social structure. The first result of Turner’s research among the Ndembu was his doctoral dissertation, later published as Schism and Continuity in an African Society: A Study of Ndembu Village Life (Turner 1957a). In this work, Turner analysed the mechanisms of resolving social conflicts in Ndembu society. The ritual theme was present, but very modestly so. Turner devoted only one of twelve chapters to the study of ritual, and it was indicatively entitled The Politically Integrative Function of Ritual. At this stage of his life, Turner saw rituals, just as he had been taught, as redressive mechanisms for the tensions produced in the secular order. Liminality Rediscovered: With Victor Turner and Beyond 77 Turner’s departure from this functionalist paradigm happened in steps, and the first such step was his introduction of the term, ‘social drama’. Turner had introduced this term because he sensed that much of social life was clearly theatrical. Anthropology therefore needed terms that would allow for a representation of culture in a more congruent way, and ‘social drama’ served this purpose well. It was also an attempt to take seriously the neo-Marxist interest in social conflict and functionalist focus on social integration, without actually employing the existing analytical vocabulary of those dominant paradigms. The social dramas among the Ndembu, Turner said, exhibit a processual form, following a pattern of four phases: (1) a breach of regular norm-governed social relationships between persons or groups of a social unit; (2) a crisis or extension of the breach, unless the conflict can be sealed off quickly; (3) adjustive and redressive mechanisms brought into operation by leading
